coredata.dc:title: Bright, blue fluorescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of folic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhifeng
Liang, Qi
Wang, Yutong
Wang, Xiao
Jing, Lulu
Cheng, Yajie
Liu, Jinqi
Jia, Zixuan
coredata.dc:description: Bright, blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) were carried out utilizing phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as raw precursor through the hydrothermal method. The as-designed N-CDs suggested excitation-dependent characteristic changing from 340 nm to 380 nm. Moreover, Folic acid could availably quench the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs. According to this experimental phenomenon, the sensitivity and selectivity of N-CDs for folic acid detection were developed. Under optimal experimental conditions, the as-prepared probe displayed excellent linearity with the folic acid concentration changing from 0.5 to 200 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.079 μM. Furthermore, the as-prepared probe indicated good sensitivity towards folic acid sensing in glucose and bovine serum samples. The recoveries of 95.9–104.8 % and 98.6–106.9 % were obtained in glucose and bovine serum samples, respectively. The corresponding relative standard deviation was lower than 2.74 % and 2.91 %, respectively. With preeminent sensitivity and selectivity, this as-developed nanosensor afforded a prospective platform for measuring folic acid in real samples. Furthermore, this N-CDs could be applied for the temperature detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Folic acid sensing
Fluorescence quenching
Temperature detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N-CDs-based Probe application in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (No. 20210302124210) and the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), size distribution (B), XRD (C) and FT-IR spectrum (D) of N-CDs.
XPS spectra of N-CDs (A), C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D).
The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (inset: picture of N-CDs solution under sunlight (left) and UV light (right)) (A); The influence of excitation on emission wavelength (B).
The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs with different folic acid concentration (A); The linear curve of F0/F and folic acid concentration (B).

The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs and N-CDs + folic acid (A); The TEM image of N-CDs + folic acid (B); The UV–vis absorption spectra of folic acid, N-CDs and N-CDs + folic acid (C); The Stern–Volmer curves of N-CDs and folic acid at different temperatures (D).
The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs under different temperature (A); the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature (B); the cycles number (C).
Comparison of different analytical methods for folic acid determination.
The folic acid detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Bright, blue fluorescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of folic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bright, blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) were carried out utilizing phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as raw precursor through the hydrothermal method. The as-designed N-CDs suggested excitation-dependent characteristic changing from 340 nm to 380 nm. Moreover, Folic acid could availably quench the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs. According to this experimental phenomenon, the sensitivity and selectivity of N-CDs for folic acid detection were developed. Under optimal experimental conditions, the as-prepared probe displayed excellent linearity with the folic acid concentration changing from 0.5 to 200 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.079 μM. Furthermore, the as-prepared probe indicated good sensitivity towards folic acid sensing in glucose and bovine serum samples. The recoveries of 95.9–104.8 % and 98.6–106.9 % were obtained in glucose and bovine serum samples, respectively. The corresponding relative standard deviation was lower than 2.74 % and 2.91 %, respectively. With preeminent sensitivity and selectivity, this as-developed nanosensor afforded a prospective platform for measuring folic acid in real samples. Furthermore, this N-CDs could be applied for the temperature detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid, as a water-soluble vitamin, is also called methoteryl glutamate due to its abundant content in green leaves [1]. There are several forms of existence in nature, and their parent compounds are formed by combining three components: pteridine, p-aminobenzoic acid, and glutamic acid. Folic acid can be actively absorbed and passively absorbed through diffusion in the body, with the absorption site mainly located in the upper part of the small intestine [2,3]. Folic acid is reduced by folate reductase with the participation of NADPH to physiologically active tetrahydrofolate (THFA or FH4), which is involved in the synthesis of purines and pyrimidines [4]. Therefore, folic acid plays an important role in protein synthesis and cell division and growth, promoting the formation of normal red blood cells. Lack of folic acid can cause a decrease in hemoglobin production in red blood cells and hinder cell maturation, leading to megaloblastic anemia. Presently, the amount of folic acid has been utilized as effective biomarkers for early diagnosis of different diseases in blood samples [5,6]. Thus, establishing a facile and rapid strategy to determine trace folic acid is imminent. Up to now, many scientific researchers have developed a series of strategies for the sensing of folic acid, including electrochemical detection [7–9], high performance liquid chromatography [10], enzymatic analysis [11], surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy [12], spectrophotometry [13], chemiluminescence [14], resonance energy transfer electrochemiluminescence [15], fluorescence [16–20]. These strategies can afford preferable accuracy and sensitivity, but the traditional required equipment are expensive and demand specialized personnel for preparation of samples and testing. Recently, different fluorescence probes, such as organic dyes, metal organic framework structure, semiconductor quantum dots and metal nanoclusters, have performed huge interest for the sensitive determination of molecules and ions because of their excellent sensitivity, simple procedures and quick detection [21–24]. However, these fluorescence sensors also possess some defects including complex preparation processes, high toxicity, poor biocompatibility and stability, which restrict their actual applications [25].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescence nanomaterial, were found in 2004. And CDs usually consists of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements [26]. The morphology of CDs usually presents a spherical shape, and their average diameters are usually not higher than 10 nm. Compared with the traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CDs display many excellent performance, including strong fluorescence, excellent water solubility, high photostability, low toxicity, larger Stokes shift and adjustability of emission wavelength [27]. The above characteristics promote CDs to have many applications such as biosensing, bioimaging, drug release, Wastewater treatment, air pollution control, energy conversion, quantum computing and optoelectronic devices [28]. Biomass and chemical molecules can usually be used to synthesize carbon dots. So far, some strategies have been developed for the preparation of CDs, such as top-down synthesis route (arc discharge method, laser ablation method, electrochemical synthesis method and incomplete combustion method) and bottom-up synthesis route (template method, hydrothermal method, solvothermal method and microwave method). Among them, due to its environmentally friendly and affordable features, the hydrothermal method has been widely applied to develop different types of CDs. However, there are still some issues with CDs, such as low fluorescence quantum yield and poor optical properties. Thus, element doping and surface modification have been gradually applied to upgrade the optical characteristics of CDs. So far, doped elements mainly include nitrogen, boron, sulfur, phosphorus, chlorine, etc [29,30]. To today, only a few CD-based fluorescent sensors for folic acid determination have been carried out. Mu et al. developed the N, S, I-doped CDs to detect folic acid, which performed a LOD (84.0 nM) for folic acid via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer [31]. From Wen group, the optical CDs developed using Hyaluronic acid was applied to rapidly determine folic acid (4.0 μM-100.0 μM) with a LOD of 0.49 μM [32]. Many previous reports generally involved complex development conditions, difficult modification procedures, and low LOD. Thus, it is still imperative to develop a high-sensitivity fluorescent sensor for folic acid determination.

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully developed through a one-pot hydrothermal method utilizing phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as the raw precursors, which integrated preparation and surface modification. N-CDs held strong blue light emission at 416 nm under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

The structure and optical behavior of N-CDs were displayed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), molecular fluorescence, UV visible absorption spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The quenching effect of folic acid on the fluorescence of N-CDs and quenching mechanism were carried out. The determination system of folic acid was developed, and the sensitivity and feasibility were explored, which could display the application of N-CDs for folic acid sensing.

O-phenylenediamine, ascorbic acid and quinine sulfate were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (China). Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, glucose, fructose, sodium citrate, citric acid, glutathione, cysteine, phenylalanine, glycine, lysine, glutamate, tyrosine, leucine, histidine and folic acid were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (China). All of the chemicals employed were analytical pure and used without further purification.

The morphology and particle size statistics of N-CDs were implemented via the JEOL JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Japan) at the voltage of 200 kV. The N-CDs were added to the copper mesh for the TEM measurement. The FT-IR data of N-CDs were carried out on the Nicolet 6700 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) at the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The FT-IR experiments were obtained through potassium bromide tablet pressing technology. The U-4500 UV–vis absorption spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) was applied for the absorption data measurement of N-CDs. The fluorescence data of N-CDs with and without folic acid were developed utilizing F-7000 type fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) within a 1 cm × 1 cm fluorescent cuvette. The ESCALAB 250 type X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) was used to discuss the composition and oxidation state of elements of N-CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were carried out on Ultima IV (Rigaku, Japan).

The development procedure of N-CDs was as follows: 0.2 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.1 g ascorbic acid were dropped into beaker containing 30 mL ultrapure water and subjected to ultrasound for 10 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into the stainless steel lined autoclave (50 mL) and placed an autoclave (180 ℃) for 6 h. Next, the stainless steel lined autoclave was placed to room temperature. The yellow solution was processed via 0.22 µM filter membrane and centrifuge (10000 rpm, 15 min). Finally, the processed solution was freeze-dried to obtain a solid sample, which would be beneficial for further analysis and testing.

The determination of folic acid was performed in (citric acid-sodium citrate) buffer solution at room temperature. First of all, the fluorescence response situation of N-CDs to folic acid was discussed. The mixed solution was prepared including 200 μL N-CDs, 1.8 mL buffer solution (pH = 6) and different concentration folic acid solution (final concentration was 200 μM). The mixed solution was placed at room temperature for 5 min. Then, the corresponding fluorescence data were obtained through F-7000 type fluorescence spectrometer at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The selectivity experiments of the N-CDs towards folic acid fluorescence determination were discussed with the addition of a series of references. The references included Na+, K+, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, glucose, fructose, citric acid, glutathione, cysteine, phenylalanine, glycine, lysine, glutamate, tyrosine, leucine and histidine, the amount of the references was 1000 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs-based Probe application in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, the glucose and bovine serum samples were used as real samples to discuss the feasibility of N-CDs as fluorescence probe for the folic acid measuring. Before sensing, the glucose and bovine serum samples were diluted with citric acid sodium citrate buffer solution (pH = 6). Then different concentrations of folic acid were added into the above solution. The correlation results were obtained through linear equation such as measured concentration, recovery and relative standard deviation.

In this paper, blue-emitting N-CDs were developed via o-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as precursors by hydrothermal synthesis method. The TEM was applied for the study of morphology and particle size distribution of N-CDs. As illustrated in

Fig. 1 A, the particle size of N-CDs were spherical and uniform, and aggregation phenomenon was not observed. The average size of N-CDs was 2.5 ± 0.062 nm by counting the particle size between 2.1 nm and 2.7 nm (Fig. 1B). Additionally, a broad peak at 2θ = 21.57° was observed in XRD diagram, exhibiting the disorder of carbon atoms (Fig. 1C) [33]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectroscopy was utilized for the study of functional groups types on the surface of N-CDs. As indicated in Fig. 1D, the stretching vibration of OH and NH were located at 3446.7 cm−1 [34]. The characteristic peaks at 2960.2 and 2901.3 cm−1 were ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of CH bond [35]. The stretching vibrations of the CO was located at 1631.8 cm−1 [36]. The stretching vibration absorption peak of CN bond was observed at 1110.8 cm−1 [37]. The deformation vibrations of the CH bond was located at 1386.3 cm−1. The infrared analysis results exhibited that some functional groups could be observed on the surface of N-CDs, such as hydroxy, amino, and carboxyl groups. These groups ensured the N-CDs possessed good water solubility, which was beneficial to develop a wider range of applications. By investigating the FT-IR image of N-CDs, the discovery of nitrogen-containing functional groups suggested that nitrogen element have been doped into the N-CDs.

The zeta potential analyzer was used to discuss the surface charge of as-developed N-CDs, and the graph was displayed in Fig. S1. Because of the existence of hydroxy, amino, and carboxyl groups, the zeta potential value of the N-CDs showed negative value and could reach −1.67 mV. Moreover, the element type and surface groups of N-CDs could be confirmed through XPS. Three characteristic peaks at 285.1 eV, 400.5 eV and 532.4 eV were observed in full XPS spectrum of N-CDs, which were designated as the peak of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2 A). Additionally, the single XPS spectrum of C1s could be divided into three characteristic peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.7 eV and 287.3 eV, which were ascribed to the peak of CC, CN and O-CO bond (Fig. 2B) [38]. The two peaks originated from the decomposition of the single XPS spectrum of N1s. These three peaks were located at 399.5 and 400.6 eV, which were attributed to CN and NH bond, respectively (Fig. 2C) [39]. As exhibited in Fig. 2D, three characteristic peaks at 530.9 eV, 531.9 eV and 533.0 eV were observed in single XPS spectrum of O1s, which were assigned to the peak of OCO, C-OH and O-CO bond [40]. The XPS and FT-IR results suggested that the nitrogen was doped into the system of N-CDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrometer and fluorescence analyzer were successfully utilized for the study of optical characteristics of N-CDs. Due to the surface state trapping the excited state energy, two absorption peaks at 241 and 298 nm were observed, which were attribute to the π–π* transition of the CC bond and the n–π* transition of the CO bond (Fig. S2) [41]. In Fig. 3 A, when the excitation was set to 365 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of N-CDs was 416 nm. The appearance color of N-CDs was inserted into Fig. 3A. The solution color of N-CDs were faint yellow and blue under sunlight and ultraviolet light, respectively. As suggested in Fig. 3B, when the change range of the excitation wavelength was set as 340–380 nm, the emission wavelength was constantly changing. Then, this characteristic exhibited that N-CDs was excitation wavelength-dependent. The feature of N-CDs was related to the particles of different sizes and distribution of different surface states [42]. The fluorescence quantum yield of the as-developed N-CDs was 18.5 %.

The stability of N-CDs was studied via UV irradiation, room temperature preservation, and addition of sodium chloride (Fig. S3). After 20 min of UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity scarcely varied. Additionally, the as-prepared N-CDs did not emerge to focculent matter, indicating the excellent stability of N-CDs. The tolerance of N-CDs towards sodium chloride was also investigated. The different sodium chloride concentration were introduced into the N-CDs solution, and the corresponding fluorescence intensities were obtained via fluorometer. When the sodium chloride concentration reached 0.05 M, the fluorescence intensity did not decrease, indicating the excellent salt resistance of N-CDs. The stability results suggested that N-CDs possessed charming application prospects.

A series of folic acid amounts were introduced into the detection system, and the corresponding fluorescence intensities were acquired to discuss the sensor’s sensitivity towards folic acid. In Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the detection system could be effectively quenched. When the folic acid concentrations increased from 0 to 200 μM, the fluorescence intensities decreased. Within the linear range of 0.5–200 μM, the linear phenomenon of fluorescence response was observed between F0/F and folic acid concentration (F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs without and with folic acid). The equation for fitting the curve was F0/F = 0.9611[Q] + 0.0158 with the fitting coefficient of 0.9892. Based on 3σ/k, the limit of detection (LOD) was suggested to be 0.079 μM (σ is the standard deviation of detection system’s fluorescence intensity, k is the slope of the fitted curve). Additionally, some indicators were compared between reported methods and the as-prepared platform. As displayed in Table 1 , the as-developed nanosensor possessed excellent sensitivity for folic acid sensing, such as lower LOD and wider linear range. Then, the stable and friendly fluorescent sensor suggested excellent sensitivity for folic acid detection and was suitable for detecting folic acid in actual samples.

In this study, to discuss the selectivity of this as-prepared nanosensor for folic acid detection, the influence of different control substance on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-based sensor was discussed. The control substances used in this paper had Na+, K+, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, glucose, fructose, citric acid, glutathione, cysteine, phenylalanine, glycine, lysine, glutamate, tyrosine, leucine and histidine. In Fig. S4A, only folic acid could prominently weaken the blue fluorescence of N-CDs-based sensor. After the addition of control substances, the quenching efficiency could be ignored. This result was mainly attributed to the non-specific binding sites on the surface of N-CDs. These all results indicate that N-CDs had better binding capacity for folic acid due to the specific cavities left by the removal of the template. As indicated in Fig. S4B, when the folic acid was added into the N-CDs with control substances, the effect of control substances on the sensor’s blue fluorescence was negligible. The above experimental results indicated that the N-CDs-based nanoprobe could selectively determine folic acid in the samples.

The mechanism of N-CDs as the fluorescence nanosensors for the sensing of the folic acid was discussed via a series of related experiments. To inform the fluorescence quenching mechanism, we carried out the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs and N-CDs + folic acid mixture. It could be seen from Fig. 5 A, based on the formula τ=(B1*t1 2 + B2*t2 2)/(B1*t1 + B2*t2), the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs and N-CDs + folic acid mixture were 5.43 ns and 5.39 ns, exhibiting that the changes could be ignored. The value of fluorescence lifetime exhibited that the static quenching was the main mechanism. In Fig. 5B, an aggregation phenomenon can be observed via the TEM image of N-CDs with the addition of folic acid, implying that the existence of aggregation induced effects. Due to the presence of amino and carboxyl groups on N-CDs and folic acid, hydrogen bonds were easily formed, which would help generate complex between N-CDs and folic acid. As shown in Fig. 5C, compared with UV–vis absorption spectra of folic acid and N-CDs, a peak at 361 nm disappeared in UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs + folic acid. Furthermore, the linear relationship between F0/F and folic acid concentration was discussed at different temperatures. As suggested in Fig. 5D, the slope of the curve decreased as the temperature increased. Again, this confirmed that the reaction mechanism between N-CDs and folic acid was primarily a static quenching process [43].

To validate the practicability of the N-CDs-based fluorescence probe in real samples, we utilized this nanosnesor for the measurement of folic acid in the glucose and bovine serum samples. Folic acid with different amounts were introduced into the processed glucose and bovine serum samples, and then the corresponding fluorescence intensities were carried out via fluorometer. The measured concentrations were obtained through the linear fitting equation. As displayed in Table 2 , folic acid could be determined in spiked real samples, the recovery of folic acid was in the range of 95.9–106.9 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower 2.91 %. These results displayed that the as-developed N-CDs could be applied as efficient fluorescence sensors for the determination of folic acid in real samples.

In Fig. 6 A, when the temperature increased from 15 to 95 ℃, the fluorescence intensities decreased. Within the linear range of 15–95 ℃, the linear phenomenon of fluorescence response was observed between F and temperature (F represented the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs). The equation for fitting the curve was F = -7.15[Tem] + 1086 with the fitting coefficient of 0.9953 (Fig. 6B). Meanwhile, the N-CDs could detect temperature multiple times without significant decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6C).

Doping of elements are effective way to upgrade the fluorescence characteristic of carbon dots. From this strategy, the nitrogen was used as a doping element to develop CDs through hydrothermal method. With the excitation wavelength changing from 340 nm to 380 nm, the emission wavelength did not have a fixed value. Additionally, this as-developed N-CDs possessed excellent UV radiation stability and salt resistance. In the selective study, only folic acid could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs. The quenching mechanism was attributed to the static quenching. Additionally, we utilized this nanoprobe for the determination of folic acid in real samples, and the correlation results displayed excellent reproducibility and accuracy.

Zhifeng Cai: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology. Qi Liang: . Yutong Wang: Methodology, Visualization. Xiao Wang: Software, Validation. Lulu Jing: Software, Validation. Yajie Cheng: Methodology, Visualization. Jinqi Liu: Software, Validation. Zixuan Jia: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (No. 20210302124210) and the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemphys.2024.112216.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
